title: Discipleship 7: The Husband as Discipler
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 7 - The Husband as Discipler - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Marriage as a Discipleship Relationship
subpoints: Overview of series: marriage, parenting, and church discipleship
The pattern established: wife disciples husband, husband disciples wife
Today's focus: the role of the husband, beginning with Christ as the pattern
Why distortions of marriage are so serious: marriage images Christ and the church

outline.title: Christ as Head of the Church
subpoints: Two meanings of 'head': source (head of a river) and authoritative ruler
Paul's primary meaning: Christ as authoritative ruler over the church (Eph 1:22-23)
The church only does what her Head says — implication: the regulative principle of worship
Christ gives gifts and institutes church government by elders
The husband as head images Christ; every action makes a statement about Christ

outline.title: Christ as Savior and Lover of the Church
subpoints: Christ is savior of the body — the church is his body; no salvation outside the church
Visible church exists in local representation, not as an abstraction
Christ loved the church in the past (gave himself) — pointing to the cross
Christ gave himself in the place of the church; demonstrated love while we were still sinners
Purpose: to sanctify and cleanse her with the washing of water (baptism) and present her glorious

outline.title: The Glorious Church and the Present Ministry of Christ
subpoints: Christ plays the long game: presenting the church to himself at his return
Glorious defined negatively (no spot or wrinkle) and positively (holy and blameless)
Holiness — not size, relevance, or programs — is what makes a church glorious
Christ currently nourishes and cherishes the church
Revelation 19:6-9: the wedding feast of the Lamb as the culmination

outline.title: The Visible Church, Church Membership, and Conclusion
subpoints: The Abrahamic covenant as the founding structure of the visible church
Three marks of a faithful church present with Abraham: Word, sacrament, discipline
Church membership as a biblical concept throughout Scripture (Acts 2, 5; 1 Corinthians 5; 2 Corinthians 2)
Submission to church authority implies formal membership
Husbands as images of Christ: the dignity, privilege, and weight of the calling

summary:
In this seventh session of the Discipleship series, Pastor Tito Lyro introduces the role of the husband as a discipler in marriage. Before addressing the husband specifically, he takes a step back to examine what Paul says about Christ and the church in Ephesians 5, because the husband's role is explicitly modeled on that relationship. The lesson establishes that marriage was instituted from creation to illustrate Christ's love for his church, and that any distortion of marriage — whether through same-sex marriage, divorce, polygamy, or the more subtle distortions common inside the church (role reversal, lack of love, refusal to submit) — misrepresents Christ to the world.

The heart of the lesson is a careful exposition of Christ's relationship to the church under several headings. Christ is the head (authoritative ruler) of the church, which has practical implications for the regulative principle of worship — the church only does what her Head tells her through his Word. Christ is also the Savior of the body, meaning salvation exists only within the church, and the visible church manifests locally in faithful congregations. Christ loved the church by giving himself for her in the past (the cross), and he currently nourishes and cherishes her, working to present her as a glorious, holy, and blameless bride at his return.

The lesson then broadens into a substantial discussion of the visible church and church membership. Pastor Lyro traces membership as a biblical concept from the Abrahamic covenant (with its three marks of Word, sacrament, and discipline) through the New Testament, showing that belonging to the church has always involved a formal, covenantal act of identification. He concludes by noting that because husbands constantly image Christ in their families — whether truthfully or as a lie — understanding what Christ does for the church is foundational to understanding what godly husbands are called to do.
transcript:
## Marriage as a Discipleship Relationship

We are in the midst of our series on discipleship, looking at how marriage is itself a discipleship relationship. We have seen that the wife disciples the husband and the husband disciples the wife. In our last two lessons we examined how the wife disciples her husband. Today we follow the same pattern with the husband: first looking at his role in marriage, and then seeing how he disciples his wife.

## The Distortion of Marriage

We begin with Ephesians 5:22–33. As we read this passage, it is easy to assume that the Holy Spirit is using marriage merely to illustrate Christ's leadership of the church. But the reverse is actually true. From the very beginning, God established marriage in the garden *in order to illustrate* how the Messiah would love his church. Marriage was instituted in Genesis 2 with that purpose built into it. That is why any distortion of marriage is so serious — it misrepresents Christ and his church.

When we think about distortions of marriage, it is easy to list the obvious ones out there: same-sex marriage, divorce, polygamy, open relationships, cohabitation. We rightly oppose all of those. But we must not forget that marriage is also distorted within the church when husbands and wives fail to fulfill the biblical roles God has given them — role reversal, refusing to lead, refusing to submit. These distortions also damage the picture of Christ and the church.

## Pulling the Husband Out of the Picture: Christ and the Church

Before we talk about the husband's role directly, I want to step back and focus just on the illustration: what Paul says about Christ and the church in this passage.

Paul describes several things Christ does for the church:

- **Christ is the head of the church** (v. 23). "Head" here does not mean merely "source" but carries the meaning of ruling authority — the one who governs. Paul confirms this in Ephesians 1:22–23: the Father put all things under Christ's feet and gave him to be head over all things to the church. To have all things under your feet is the biblical image of a conquering king. So Christ is the authoritative ruler over his church. The church submits to him — she does not do her own thing — and her head speaks to her through his word. This is what theologians call the *regulative principle of worship*: worship is regulated by the word of Christ, the head of the church. Because Christ is head, there is no other head. Our confession is plain: the Lord Jesus Christ is the only head of the church, and the claim of any man to be the vicar of Christ is a dishonoring usurpation.

- **Christ is the Savior of the body** (v. 23). The church is the body of Christ. Christ is her only Savior, and there is no salvation outside the church — no such thing as someone saved by Christ who is not part of his body. The visible church, as our confession says, is the house and family of God, outside of which there is no ordinary possibility of salvation.

- **Christ loved the church and gave himself for her** (v. 25). Paul uses the past tense — *loved* — pointing to a specific act in history: the cross. God demonstrated his love for the church in this manner: he gave his only Son.

> *"But God demonstrates His own love toward us, in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us."* (Romans 5:8)

- **Christ gave himself in order to sanctify and cleanse the church** (vv. 26–27). Jesus set his bride apart through the washing of baptism and will present her to himself as a glorious church, without spot or wrinkle, holy and blameless. Notice how Paul defines "glorious": not by size, not by cultural relevance, not by numbers — only by holiness. A glorious church in Christ's eyes is a holy church.

- **Christ nourishes and cherishes the church** (v. 29). In the present tense: he is doing this now. He gives the church everything she needs to grow and tenderly cares for and protects her.

## Why This Matters for Husbands

Every husband, whether he intends it or not, is constantly saying something about Christ by the way he treats his wife. We cannot turn off this "Christ-imaging device." At every moment we are either declaring a truth about Christ or a lie about Christ. That should sober every man here.

We are beginning to see how important it is for the husband to be a godly head. We are also beginning to see how important it is for him to be Christlike in every way — as savior, as lover, as one who nourishes and cherishes. All of this is what it means for the husband to disciple his wife.

Let us pray that God would help us as husbands to understand the dignity and importance, the privilege and burden, the duties and glory of being a husband who reflects Christ truthfully to the wife and to the family.
